stories.body: Regional president Juanma Moreno said 11 people have died and 19 remain missing after a wildfire believed to have been sparked by a downed power line tore through wooded areas near Los Gallardos in Almería, with four victims found in a right-hand-drive car thought to belong to British nationals, others discovered while fleeing on foot, and about 1,000 residents evacuated as temperatures near 40C, roads closed, and Spain’s Military Emergency Unit joined more than 150 firefighters battling fast-moving flames amid a Europe-wide heatwave that has already produced record burn totals across Spain.
stories.headline: At least 11 dead and 19 missing as southern Spain wildfire devastates Almería villages

stories.body: As millions gathered in Mashhad to bury slain Supreme Leader Ayatollah Ali Khamenei, Iranian officials said they launched ballistic missiles, drones and cruise missiles at U.S. military infrastructure in Kuwait, Qatar, Bahrain and Jordan in retaliation for American strikes that Tehran says killed 14 people and hit the perimeter of the Bushehr nuclear plant, while the Revolutionary Guards warned the Strait of Hormuz will reopen only under Iranian arrangements even as mediators from Qatar, Turkey, Oman and Pakistan urge diplomacy to salvage an unraveling ceasefire and protect global shipping.
stories.headline: Iran claims strikes on U.S. Gulf bases as Khamenei is buried, warns over Hormuz control

stories.body: Three sources close to the Kremlin told Reuters that President Vladimir Putin has rebuffed advisers urging negotiations and instead sees a “high probability” of escalating the war to seize the remaining parts of Donbas, potentially even striking NATO assets to create a security justification for wider mobilization, while Ukraine says its intelligence shows Russia preparing fresh operations beyond its borders even as drone attacks on Russian refineries trigger fuel shortages and domestic polls show Putin’s approval dipping to its lowest point since the 2022 invasion.
stories.headline: Kremlin sources say Putin preparing escalation in Ukraine despite renewed U.S. pressure

stories.body: U.S. Central Command said forces shot down four Iranian drones believed to be targeting maritime traffic in the Strait of Hormuz, then struck Iranian surveillance radar sites in Goruk and on Qeshm Island as part of efforts to secure the waterway, highlighting how Iran’s near-blockade of a route that once carried a fifth of the world’s oil is complicating indirect negotiations aimed at ending the months-long war that began with February strikes by the United States and Israel.
stories.headline: U.S. hits Iranian coastal radar after drone attacks threaten Hormuz shipping lanes

stories.body: Five astronauts briefly sheltered in their SpaceX Crew Dragon lifeboat after NASA saw an air leak in Russia’s Zvezda module double from roughly one pound per day to two, but the U.S. agency rescinded the evacuation once Roscosmos sealed one of two cracks and paused planned sawing repairs that NASA engineers opposed, underscoring tensions over how to manage the 27-year-old station’s aging infrastructure even as lawmakers push to extend operations through 2032 to compete with China’s growing space presence.
stories.headline: NASA reverses ISS evacuation order after Roscosmos assesses worsening air leak

stories.body: Mexican Foreign Minister Roberto Velasco said Mexico will bring U.S. criminal complaints and civil actions over 14 Mexicans who died in Immigration and Customs Enforcement detention and three who died during ICE arrest operations, including builder Lorenzo Salgado Araujo, whose shooting by an ICE officer in Houston sparked protests and congressional calls for investigations as DHS defends the agent by alleging Salgado rammed a vehicle and used it as a weapon.
stories.headline: Mexico to file U.S. criminal complaints over citizens’ deaths in ICE custody and raids

stories.body: Progressive Democrat Graham Platner suspended his Maine Senate campaign days after Politico published Jenny Racicot’s allegation that he coerced sex five years ago, prompting national figures such as Bernie Sanders, Elizabeth Warren and Ro Khanna, as well as the DSCC and Senate Majority PAC, to withdraw support, leaving the state party until July 27 to pick a new nominee against Republican Sen. Susan Collins via a convention process Platner said should be open to grassroots input.
stories.headline: Graham Platner exits Maine Senate race after sexual assault allegation rattles Democrats

stories.body: In a 52-47 pre-dawn vote with no Democratic support, Senate Republicans approved an extra $70 billion for Immigration and Customs Enforcement and Border Patrol over three years without eliminating former President Donald Trump’s $1.8 billion “anti-weaponization” compensation fund for allies, despite bipartisan amendments seeking to kill or repurpose it and warnings from moderates like Susan Collins that relying solely on acting Attorney General nominee Todd Blanche’s assurances leaves the politically toxic fund intact ahead of November’s elections.
stories.headline: Senate passes $70 billion ICE funding bill while preserving Trump’s controversial ally fund

stories.body: The Associated Press tallied at least eight deaths linked to recent U.S. immigration raids, highlighting grieving families from Houston to Minneapolis and raising alarms among activists who cite cases such as the killing of Alex Pretti by Border Patrol and Jaime Alanis’s death during farmworker operations as evidence that Homeland Security’s aggressive tactics require independent scrutiny and stronger accountability measures.
stories.headline: Houston raid shooting is eighth fatality tied to federal immigration sweeps, fueling oversight demands

stories.body: Junta chief Juan Manuel Moreno unveiled his first government with far-right Vox by handing spokesman Manuel Gavira a vice presidency over tourism, deregulation, justice and local administration, but he simultaneously created two additional vice presidencies for trusted Popular Party allies Antonio Sanz and Carolina España so that, even in Moreno’s absence, control remains with his core team that retains the health, emergency, economy and finance portfolios.
stories.headline: Andalusian president expands cabinet to include Vox while bolstering loyalists with new vice presidencies

stories.body: Madrid’s Prosecutor’s Office filed a 25-page brief asking the jury court to absolve Prime Minister Pedro Sánchez’s wife Begoña Gómez, her Moncloa adviser Cristina Álvarez and businessman Juan Carlos Barrabés, insisting the investigation opened by Judge Juan Carlos Peinado—who even seized Gómez’s passport—describes no conduct that constitutes an offense and therefore, in the prosecution’s words, “without a crime there is no author.”
stories.headline: Prosecutors demand Begoña Gómez acquittal, arguing judge’s case lacks any crime or culprit

stories.body: A new UCO report to National Court Judge Santiago Pedraz implicates former Correos president Juan Manuel Serrano—once Pedro Sánchez’s chief of staff—in schemes tied to the Leire Díez case that allegedly manipulated SEPI contracts and sought to “destabilize” judicial proceedings affecting the PSOE and government, stating there are indications he participated both in business-side profiteering and in shielding specific party figures.
stories.headline: Civil Guard links former Correos president to alleged plot targeting PSOE-related court cases

stories.body: Three days after the Teodora Lamadrid street collapse forced the evacuation of 93 homes, Generalitat and Barcelona City Hall told neighbors that consolidation and compaction work between the cavity and the L9 tunnel will now run nonstop with the Guardia Urbana holding building keys to ease repeated access, while technical inspections show no structural pathologies so far, auscultation data indicate settling is slowing, and residents across Sant Gervasi remain anxious about the tunneling machine’s next moves and whether insurers will cover any new damage.
stories.headline: Barcelona orders 24/7 stabilization of Putxet sinkhole as residents await Saturday safety verdict

stories.body: Workers represented by Intersindical and CGT voted at Biblioteca Can Fabra to keep their indefinite strike going all summer after talks with the city stalled, leaving users unsure which branches are open as closures have shifted from 78% of libraries in the first week to about 27% now, and unions warn that without better staffing, pay parity with other municipal employees, and reliable climate refuge access, services and vulnerable visitors will keep suffering.
stories.headline: Barcelona’s public libraries extend indefinite strike through the summer over staffing demands

stories.body: Barcelona’s municipal government launched a summer hiring round for 550 public-sector positions ranging from 146 subalterns and 69 administrative assistants to specialist roles such as 37 senior legal officers, 31 mid-level social workers, 25 special-education technicians, 16 ICT experts, 14 psychologists, multiple architects, engineers and conductors, plus singular posts in veterinary medicine, public health and biblioteconomy, with applications due by July 28 via the Diputació’s official job portal.
stories.headline: City of Barcelona opens 550 municipal job vacancies across 40 professional profiles
